In chapter 2, we encounter a remarkable silence. God's name appears nowhere in the book of Esther, and yet His hand moves with unmistakable precision. A Jewish exile named Mordecai, far from Jerusalem, raises an orphaned girl named Hadassah—renamed Esther. There is nothing extraordinary about their circumstances. They are displaced persons in a foreign empire, living under a pagan king whose whim determines their fate. When the royal edict summons young women to the palace, Esther is taken into the harem like any other. She doesn't volunteer; she is conscripted. Yet in every room she enters, she finds favor. With Hegai, the eunuch in charge. With the other women. Eventually, with King Xerxes himself. The text insists on this repeatedly—she "obtained his favor," she "pleased him," everyone "saw her" and found her favorable. This is not the outcome of her own scheming or ambition. She even follows Mordecai's instruction to conceal her Jewish identity, revealing no strategic advantage for herself. She simply finds favor, and favor leads to the crown.

What strikes us here is the hiddenness of God's work. No prophet announces a plan. No burning bush appears. God does not speak, and yet events conspire with astonishing coordination. A girl from the exile becomes queen. Mordecai positions himself at the palace gate. A conspiracy to assassinate the king is uncovered and documented. Each piece falls into place not because anyone is consciously serving God's purposes, but simply because people act and favor flows. This is the reality that the rest of Esther's story will make clear: God governs all things, often invisibly, often through ordinary people making ordinary choices, even in kingdoms where His name is never invoked.

But we should not mistake this hiddenness for absence. The text is teaching us something crucial about faith in a world that often feels abandoned by God. Esther lives in exile, separated from the Temple, unable to observe many of the laws given to her people. She is in danger simply by existing—her identity must be concealed. Yet she is not left without a guardian in Mordecai, who watches her welfare daily, who guides her choices with wisdom, who is positioned to protect her when the moment comes. God has not abandoned His people even when they are dispersed among nations that do not acknowledge Him. The question the book poses to us is whether we will trust in His invisible providence, whether we will obey the counsel of those who fear Him, whether we will recognize His hand when it moves not with thunder but with the quiet coordination of favor, timing, and circumstance.
